coredata.dc:title: A novel electrochemical aptasensor for highly sensitive and quantitative detection of the streptomycin antibiotic
coredata.dc:creator: Ghanbari, Kazhal
Roushani, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, we report a facile approach to employ gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) and thiol graphene quantum dots (GQD-SH) as the nanomaterial for ultrasensitive detection of streptomycin (STR). Based on this strategy, a GQD-SH was immobilized onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). AuNPs have been immobilized on SH groups of GQDs through bonding formation of AuS and Apt have been loaded on the electrode surface through the interaction between thiol group of aptamer. By incubating STR as a target onto the surface of the prepared Apt/AuNPs/GQD-SH/GCE as a proposed nanoaptasensor, the Apt/STR complex was formed and the changes of the electrochemical signal were evaluated with the EIS technique. The proposed nanoaptasensor showed wide linear range from 0.1 to 700pgml−1. Finally, the proposed nanoaptasensor was successfully applied for the determination of STR in real samples and satisfactory results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptamer
Gold nanoparticle
Thiol graphene quantum dots
Streptomycin

Optimization of analytical conditions and incubation time of streptomycin nanoaptasensor
Electrochemical response of the aptasensor to streptomycin
Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the proposed nanoaptasensor
The assay application in real sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nyquist curves of the recorded modified electrode for each steps in: GCE (curve a), GQD-SH/GCE (curve b), AuNPs/GQD-SH/GCE (curve c), Apt/AuNPs/GQD-SH/GCE (curve d), BSA/Apt/AuNPs/GQD-SH/GCE (curve e), Inset is the equivalent circuit. at the frequency range of 0.1Hz–10kHz.
dependences of peak currents on incubation time of nanotools.

A) EISs obtained after incubating with different concentrations of the STR from 0.1pg·ml−1 to 700pg·ml−1 in 0.1M KCl containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and B) calibration curve of the sensor for detecting STR. y=0.0395x+18.123 R2 =0.9921.
The response peak intensity for 5 electrodes which modified under the same conditions.

The DPV results of changes of the proposed aptasensor response to interferes such as amoxicillin (red line), ciprofloxacin (green line), gentamicin (purple line), at the concentration of 10ng·ml−1 for each interferences in the presence of 100pg·ml−1 of STR (blue line).
Schematic illustration of the proposed electrochemical aptasensor for streptomycin detection.
Analytical parameters for aptasensors have been recently used for streptomycin detection.
Detection of streptomycin in serum samples.

head.ce:title: A novel electrochemical aptasensor for highly sensitive and quantitative detection of the streptomycin antibiotic

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Ilam, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, we report a facile approach to employ gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) and thiol graphene quantum dots (GQD-SH) as the nanomaterial for ultrasensitive detection of streptomycin (STR). Based on this strategy, a GQD-SH was immobilized onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). AuNPs have been immobilized on SH groups of GQDs through bonding formation of AuS and Apt have been loaded on the electrode surface through the interaction between thiol group of aptamer. By incubating STR as a target onto the surface of the prepared Apt/AuNPs/GQD-SH/GCE as a proposed nanoaptasensor, the Apt/STR complex was formed and the changes of the electrochemical signal were evaluated with the EIS technique. The proposed nanoaptasensor showed wide linear range from 0.1 to 700pgml−1. Finally, the proposed nanoaptasensor was successfully applied for the determination of STR in real samples and satisfactory results were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Streptomycin (STR), an aminoglycoside antibiotic, is used in human and veterinary for treatment of gram-negative infections [1–3]. However, incorrect and uncontrolled application of STR can cause contamination along food chains and also serious side effects on human health due to allergic reactions, loss of hearing and kidney toxicity [4–6]. Toxic effects could occur when the plasma concentration of STR reaches above 35μg/ml. According to commission, maximum acceptable residues of STR in milk is 200μg/ml [7,8]. Thus, development of sensitive and selective methods for detection of STR residue in serum and animal derived foods are in great demand both in the field of environmental analysis and food safety control. As listed in Table 1 , several analytical approaches on applying aptamers have been introduced for the detection of STR accurately. Therefore, we attempted to develop an aptasensor for the precise detection of STR. Nanomaterials are promising candidates as basic building blocks and signaling elements for fabrication of biosensors with great sensitivity. In the past few years, different nanomaterials have been integrated into aptamer-based biosensors [9–12]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a unique class of nanoparticles (NPs). Due to such unique properties such as suitable conductivity, large surface to volume ratio, good biocompatibility, chemical stability, and low-toxicity, GQDs have been demonstrated to be efficient in biosensing applications [13–16]. Noble metal nanoparticles such as gold nanoparticles, platinum nanoparticle, and silver nanoparticle, are the kinds of nanomaterials that have been developed to improve the sensitivity and performance of electrochemical immunosensor and aptasensor [17–19]. Of the various metals, gold (Au) due to its unique properties such as high electrical conductivity, biological compatibility, conductive coating for biological specimens and applicability in medicine and biosensing is a suitable choice. It seems that utilizing AuNPs along with GQDs as a specific platform can be a good choice in designing electrochemical sensors and aptasensors [20,21]. In this study, we developed, a novel and highly selective aptasensor on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for the ultrasensitive detection of STR. AuNPs can strongly interact with thiol functionalized GQD (GQD-SH) and can be utilized as an efficient nanocomposite to immobilize aptamer in the construction of aptasensor. Moreover, large amounts of aptamer can be immobilized on the GQD-SH due to its large specific surface area and good biocompatibility. In the proposed method, thiol-aptamer (SH-Apt) was covalently attached to the AuNPs which was attached to the surface of the GCE modified by the GQD-SH.

The aptamer (Apt) were custom-synthesized by Bioneer (South Korea), and its sequence are given as follows: 5′-TAG GGA ATT CGT CGA CGG ATC CGG GGT CTG GTG TTC TGC TTT GTT CTG TCG GGT CGT CTG CAG GTC GAC GCA TGC GCC G-Thiol-3′. All chemicals used in the study were of analytical reagent grade with high purity. Streptomycin, dimethylformamide (DMF), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4) and all other reagents were ordered from commercial suppliers (Sigma-Aldrich, Merck or Fluka Company). All substance solutions with various concentrations were prepared by direct dissolution in 0.1M phosphate buffer (PB) solution (in pH7.4) that was prepared using Na2HPO4. In addition, a solution containing 5mM K3Fe (CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 at a ratio of 1:1 and 0.1M KCl for the experiments was applied. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Spectroscopic characterizations of GQD-SH were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer) and UV–Vis spectroscopy (VARIAN 300 Bio CARY). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations were performed using an electron microscope (Hitachi H-800). The fluorescence spectra were obtained through a Carry Eclipse (Agilent, Fluorescence and Phosphorescence spectrometer). Cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out with a μ-AUTOLAB electrochemical system type III in conjunction with FRA2 board computer controlled Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Eco-Chemio, Switzerland) driven with NOVA software. A modified GCE was used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference, and a Pt wire was used as the counter electrode. The DPV measurements were performed in 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM K3Fe (CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 with 50ms pulse period, 0.5s pulse width, 25mV step height, and 9mV potential steps. The EIS measurements were recorded within the frequency range of 0.1 to 10kHz with 5mV amplitude.

The preparation of the GQD-SH was carried out according to the literature [22], but with some modifications. Briefly, 0.1g multi walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) powder was added to 10ml of HNO3/H2SO4 mixture at a ratio of 1:3 and was sonicated for 6h at 40°C to achieve oxidized multi walled carbon nanotubes (ox-MWCNTs). The obtained black suspension containing ox-MWCNTs was diluted with distillated water and allowed to stand overnight for precipitation. Then the supernatant was removed and the suspension of ox-CNT was diluted with deionized water and filtered with a filter membrane (0.05μm) under vacuum. In order to form stable colloidal suspensions, the ox-CNT were dissolved in plenty of solvents including water, ethanol and dimethylformamide. An amount of 1.00ml of 1.00mM cysteamine solution in ethanol was added to the resulting suspension, mixed (24h), and then decanted overnight. Cysteamine leads to the isolation of NPs which is called oxidized GQD (ox-GQD) on the CNTs suspension. In the next step, 10.00mM of DCC solution in DMF was added to the collected ox-GQD to start the coupling reaction and it was mixed for 24h (at room temperature). Finally, the GQD-SH were purified by repeated filtration through a cut-off membrane (MW 1500) and rinsed with fresh DMF. In this way, GQD-SH was achieved.

For this purpose, AuNPs were synthesized according to the literature [23,24] with some modifications. In a typical procedure, 500ml of HAuCl4 (0.01% w/v) was heated with stirring to boiling point in a 1L round-bottom flask equipped with a condenser. Abruptly, 7.5ml of an aqueous solution of Na3C6H5O7.2H2O (1% w/v) was added to this solution. After 25s, the color of the solution turned to blue and finally changed to red–violet (after 70s) and boiling continued for an additional 10min. When the color of the obtained solution had turned to deep red, the heating source was removed. After 15min stirring, a homogenous colloidal solution of AuNPs with transparent red color was obtained without any precipitate. The prepared AuNPs were stored in a brown glass bottle when not in use (at 4°C). We used UV–Vis absorption spectra and TEM image to reveal the morphology of AuNP synthesis.

Before fabrication, GCE was polished down to mirror-like status with alumina powder and then sonicated in a mixture of water and ethanol (1:1) for 5min. After drying at ambient temperature, the cleaned electrode was covered by 10μl of GQD-SH solution and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, solution of AuNPs was dropped onto the surface of the modified GCE. After 3h, AuNPs was attached to thiol groups of the GQD-SH/GCE surface. Afterwards, a 10μl of Apt (2μM) was dropped on the surface of the AuNPs/GQD-SH/GCE overnight (at 4°C) so as to immobilize Apt molecules on the surface of gold nanoparticles by chemisorption between gold nanoparticles and thiol groups of Apt. Then, 10μl of BSA (1%) solution was used to cover the surface of electrode to reduce the effective surface area and available active sites and avoid non-specific adsorption. The resulting modified electrode was washed with PBS three times to eliminate the unbounded substance and stored at 4°C before use. By incubation of different STR concentrations, the Apt/STR complex was formed on the modified electrode surface. Changes in the electrochemical signal were monitored by EIS as the measuring technique. The fabrication process, stability and selectivity of the proposed nanoaptasensor were studied by CV, DPV and EIS as the effective electrochemical techniques. Schematic 1 shows the schematic representation for the fabrication of the nanoaptasensor.

Several techniques were applied to investigate the successful synthesis of GQD-SH. Spectroscopic characterizations of GQD-SH were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, fluorescence and UV–VIS spectroscopy. The morphology of the structure of GQD-SH, was recorded by TEM. The results are shown in Fig. S1. According to Fig. S1 A, the absorbance band at 3508cm−1 is attributed to hydroxyl groups in the GQD-SH; also, the bands at 2862 and 2933cm−1 are related to aliphatic CH bands, respectively. Also, the presence of an absorbance band at 2507cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of GQD-SH showed successful functionalization of the GQD with thiol group which is in line with the literature result. The TEM image in Fig. S1 B shows that the GQD-SH particle size is about 100nm in diameter, which could serve as a good path way for increasing the surface area of the modified electrode. In addition, Fig. S1 C and S1 D shows the UV–Vis absorption peak in DMF and Fluorescent emission peak, respectively. An absorption band at around 370nm and an intense emission is in agreement with other results reported in literature [22]. All these tests performed prove the successful synthesis of GQD-SH.

The synthetic AuNPs were characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy and TEM. As shown in Fig. S2 A, the existence of an absorption peak at 520nm confirms the successful synthesis of AuNPs. Determination of the size and concentration of the as-prepared AuNPs can be inferred from the UV–Vis spectra [25]. Moreover, the TEM image in Fig. S2 B shows that Au spherical nanoparticle size was found to have an average diameter of less than 8nm. This result provides additional evidence that the prepared NPs were successfully synthesized. The small size of the AuNPs leads to a strong interaction between the SH2 groups at the end of using Apt and AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the fabricated sensor
Optimization of analytical conditions and incubation time of streptomycin nanoaptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental conditions are important parameters for a high electrochemical response, which strongly influence the analytical performance. Given that Apt is a biological molecule, chemical environment must be in a way that the Apt will not be destroyed. Therefore, a neutral environment is essential. So, all of the experiments were carried out in a PB solution with pH of 7.4. This value is in line with the literature [12,26,27]. The incubation time is an important parameter for complex formation between aptamer and the target. The effect of incubation time on the aptasensor is shown in Fig. 2 , which indicates the change of current response with the increase in incubation time in target solution. With increase in incubation time from 30 to 180min, the current signal of DPV as an aptasensor response is decreased. After 120min, no further significant decrease occurred when increasing the incubation time. Thus, 120min was chosen as the optimum incubation time. This value is in accordance with the literature [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response of the aptasensor to streptomycin
ce:section.ce:para: EIS measurement was also applied as an efficient alternative detection system to further confirm the analytical performance of the proposed system. Under optimal condition, the modified electrodes were incubated in different concentrations of STR, for120 min. As shown in Fig. 3 , Rct value increased with increase in the concentration of STR, which indicated the formation of the STR/Apt complex on the modified electrode surface, which induced folding and conformational change in the Apt structure. Thus, the eT of the redox probe was prevented from the electrode surface. The Rct was proportional to the STR concentration at concentration range of 0.1–700.0pgml−1, the linear equation could be depicted as ΔRct =0.0395 [STR] (pgml−1)+18.123 and the correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9921, and also a detection limit (LOD) of 0.033pgml−1 (based on S/N=3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the proposed nanoaptasensor
The assay application in real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed sensing system was used to detect the recoveries of different concentrations of STR in human serum samples with obtained satisfactory results. Human serum samples were ordered from a local clinical laboratory and subjected to ultrafiltration by loading into a centrifugal filtration tube at 3000rpm (30min). Afterwards, the serum samples were diluted 50 times with PB solution (0.1M). The standard addition method was used for STR detection. As presented in Table 2 , the recovery indicates that the fabricated nanoaptasensor can be used for quantitative detection of streptomycin in real samples.

In conclusion, we have developed a simple and label-free electrochemical aptasensor utilizing AuNPs/GQD-SH/nanocomposite coated on glassy carbon electrode as a promising transduction platform on the electrode surface for the detection of streptomycin. GQD-SH was successfully synthesized and used to modify the working electrode. AuNPs have been immobilized onto the SH groups of GQDs via bonding formation of AuS and Aptamers have been loaded on the electrode surface via the interaction between thiol group of aptamer and AuNPs. In addition, the sensor was further examined to recognize the target specifically in human serum to investigate its potential in clinical usage. To further clarify the amplification of the proposed aptasensor towards streptomycin detection, the response characteristics of the developed aptasensor was compared to those of other streptomycin sensor. The results presented in Table 1 obviously shows that the performance of the developed sensor was superior to that of the other methods reported in the related literature, especially in comparison to different characteristics such as sensitivity, detection limit, simplicity of the fabrication procedure and linear concentration range of the proposed strategy for streptomycin biosensing which are better in some cases or comparable with the other sensor reported so far.
This study has been supported by the Ilam University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioelechem.2017.11.006.
